Well, the point of this branch is to prefix odoo modules names (e.g. `sale`,  `sale_stock`, ...) with `odoo.addons.` (e.g. `odoo.addons.sale`, `odoo.addons.sale_stock`, ...) (or `openerp.addons` for 9.0 and 8.0).

If you ignore it for version 8.0, it means the modules will be imported in the main global namespace. Generally this is not so much of an issue, as odoo looks for them both in the global namespace and in `odoo/openerp.addons` (for backward compatibility reasons). But some community addons (such as connector) rely on the modules path to be the full one, so their tests would not pass.

I don't think that we want to override `has_option`. Allowing `has_option` to cascade to `self.defaults` muddies the difference between set options and default options.

Alternatively, if we want to adopt the semantics that `has_option` will return `True` if we are able to retrieve the option, we would want to include all possible means for retrieving an option (env var, cmd, defaults, etc.)

Probably, best to just not override `has_option` and leave it meaning that the config _explicitly_ has the option.

Alright.  Thanks for pointing this out, as it alerted me to another change I had forgotten to document.  Previously, setting the time_step was done only to the phase screens themselves.  With the new changes, there's still a time_step for the phase screens that's  used when boiling, but there's also a completely independent time_step used when creating the PSF.  I.e., boiling updates and PSF time integration steps no longer need to be synchronized, and in fact individual layers may have different boiling time_steps.

So I went through and documented that fact, and also added requirements that the screen time_step only be set (different from the new default of `None`) if boiling is turned on.

I did add something like your suggested text to the time_step documentation for makePSF and PhaseScreenPSF.

This needs a lot more explanation at the start of this docstring.  "Interleaving" images is not a standard thing to do to astronomical images, so you need explain what it is doing right at the start.  Here is a straw man for you to edit:

Interleaves the pixel values from two or more images into a single larger image.  This routine is used to convert a list of images taken at a series of dither offsets into a single higher resolution image, where the value in each final pixel is the observed pixel value from exactly one of the original images.  It can be used to build a Nyquist-sampled image from images that were observed with pixels larger than the Nyquist scale.

In the original observed images, the integration of the surface brightness over the pixels is equivalent to convolution by the pixel profile and then sampling at the centers of the pixels.  This procedure thus simulates an observation sampled at a higher resolution than the original images, but still with the same pixel convolution.

One of the advantages of this procedure over other procedures that build a higher resolution image from a set of low resolution images, such as drizzle, is that the noise in the final image is not correlated.  Each pixel value comes from just a single input image.  The noise properties of the pixels are thus preserved.

A significant disadvantage is that the input images must be uniformly spaced in both x and y, which is difficult to achieve with real observations, but which can be precisely achieved with a series of simulated images.

If I call `execute_sql` a second time before the first one has returned the transaction id, I will end up creating two transaction ids which is incorrect. You can fix this by either:
- Blocking sending out the second call till you have the transaction id from the first call. But the issue with that is what if the first call for some reason takes a long time to return while the second call would have returned faster.
- Explicitly calling BeginTransaction and blocking sending out any calls till begin transaction has returned. This is the approach we took in Java client to get around the first issue.
In any case you need to block till you have a transaction id.

Not necessarily. PythonRepos are needed both by the python backend and by core pants (in the plugin resolver), so they need to be in core Pants. And PythonRepos represents a clear concept that indeed has nothing specifically to do with the python backend.

PythonSetup, OTOH, is a bit of a hodge-podge of things, but most of them do relate specifically to the Python backend (e.g., how to set up an interpreter cache). So as long as the interpreter cache is in the Python backend and not the core, PythonSetup should be there too.

And indeed, the only thing the core needed from PythonSetup was that cache ttl setting, and that usage was pretty spurious - there's no reason to link "how pants resolves its plugins" with "how pants resolves code it builds".

```

def append_file(build, file_paths):
    # The version line must have the form
    # __version__ = 'ver'
    pattern = r"^(__version__ = ['\"])([^'\"]*)(['\"])"
    repl = r"\g<1>\g<2>.dev{0}\g<3>".format(build)
    version_file = os.path.join(PROJECT_ROOT, *file_paths)
    print(
        'Updating version in version_file "{0}" with build "{1}"'
        .format(version_file, build)
    )
    replace(version_file, pattern, repl, flags=re.M)

def main(args):
    skip = args.skip
    build = args.build
    file_paths = args.file_paths

    if skip:
        print(
            'Not updating version for this build, `skip` set to "{0}"'
            .format(skip)
        )
    else:
        append_file(build, file_paths)
```
